title: Logical Operators
description: Combine conditions with AND, OR, NOT.

slides.title: Boolean Logic 🔀
slides.content:
# Logical Operators

Logical operators work with boolean values and return a boolean result. They are often used in conditions.

*   `and`  or `&&`  – Logical AND: true if both are true
*   `or`   or `||`  – Logical OR: true if at least one is true
*   `xor`  – Exclusive OR: true if exactly one is true
*   `!`    – Logical NOT: inverts the boolean

slides.title: AND and OR
slides.content:
```php
<?php
$a = true;
$b = false;

var_dump($a && $b);  // bool(false)
var_dump($a || $b);  // bool(true)

// Note: and/or have lower precedence than &&/||, so be careful with mixing.
$result = $a and $b; // assigns $a to $result, then evaluates $a and $b (but result not used)
var_dump($result);   // bool(true) – because = has higher precedence than 'and'
?>
```
slides.codeSnippet:
<?php
$a = true;
$b = false;

var_dump($a && $b);
var_dump($a || $b);

$result = $a and $b;
var_dump($result);
?>

slides.title: NOT and XOR
slides.content:
# Negation and Exclusive OR

```php
<?php
$x = true;
$y = false;

var_dump(!$x);      // bool(false) – not true is false
var_dump(!$y);      // bool(true)  – not false is true

var_dump($x xor $y); // bool(true)  – exactly one true
var_dump($x xor $x); // bool(false) – both true, so false
?>
```

quizData.question: What is the result of `(true && false) || true`?
quizData.explanation: `true && false` is false, then `false || true` is true.
